content:
Classification evaluation borrowed language from **signal detection theory** in the mid-20th century. Radar and psychology researchers distinguished **hits**, **false alarms**, **misses**, and **correct rejections**—the same four cells modern ML calls **TP, FP, FN, TN**.

**Precision** and **recall** entered information retrieval in the 1970s as **precision** (fraction of retrieved documents that are relevant) and **recall** (fraction of relevant documents retrieved). The **F-measure** popularized by **van Rijsbergen (1979)** combined them via the **harmonic mean** so neither rate could be ignored—an idea carried into **TREC** benchmarks and early **spam filtering** competitions.

**Medical diagnostics** parallel tracks used **sensitivity** (recall) and **specificity** (true negative rate), while **positive predictive value** aligned with precision. Machine learning texts in the 1990s–2000s unified these under the confusion-matrix view for **binary classifiers**.

**scikit-learn** and similar libraries standardized `precision_score`, `recall_score`, and `f1_score` with explicit `average` modes for multiclass work. Modern **imbalanced learning** and **LLM evaluation** revived precision–recall curves and macro-F1 for rare events, but the core threshold-based definitions remain unchanged.

timeline.label: Signal detection framing
timeline.description: Hits and false alarms formalize the four-way outcome table underlying precision and recall.

timeline.label: IR precision & recall
timeline.description: Information retrieval defines precision/recall for ranked document sets—later mapped to classification.

timeline.label: F-measure (1979)
timeline.description: Harmonic mean of precision and recall becomes a standard combined score in search evaluation.

timeline.label: Library-era metrics
timeline.description: scikit-learn exposes P/R/F1 with macro, micro, and weighted averaging for multiclass pipelines.
